answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe exactly where on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or trauma to your elbow?
answers.intake_a3: no, but the pain started when I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: icing on the first day and it helped a little
answers.intake_q7: Any difficulty gripping or lifting objects with your hand?
answers.intake_a7: no, just pain with lifting objects
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_q9: Any prior history of elbow problems or arthritis?
answers.intake_q10: Can you describe what types of movements or activities make the elbow pain worse or better?
answers.intake_a10: tennis makes it worse and rest makes it better

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began without trauma but started during tennis playing and worsens with the activity, while rest improves it. She denies swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, fever, or systemic symptoms. She has no difficulty gripping or lifting objects but experiences pain when lifting. Initial icing provided some relief. There is no prior history of elbow problems or arthritis. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vitals available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by pain over the lateral elbow exacerbated by repetitive wrist extension and gripping activities such as tennis. This is a common overuse injury involving the extensor tendons of the forearm.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; patient currently has no known renal or liver disease. Avoid NSAIDs in pregnancy (not applicable here). 
- Return precautions: Advise patient to return if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, weakness, swelling, redness, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given typical presentation; imaging or further studies if symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks or if diagnosis is unclear.

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis | RA | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: joints. RA affects people differently. You might have the disease for only a short time, or your symptoms might come and go. The severe form can be chronic (long-lasting) and may last a lifetime. Symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis may include: Joint pain or stiffness when moving. This is usually worse in the morning or after inactivity. RA usually affects both…

evidence.title: Juvenile Arthritis | Still's Disease | JRA | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: oms can vary depending on the type of JA. The symptoms of most types include joint pain, swelling, warmth, and stiffness. Your child may not complain of pain or stiffness, but one early sign of JA may be limping or clumsiness in the morning or after resting. Larger joints, such as the knee, may be swollen. Certain types of JA can cause a high fever , swollen…
